title: Capitalism from Below: Markets and Institutional Change in China By Victor Nee and Sonja Opper. Harvard University Press. 2012. 431 pages. $45 cloth
abstract: Capitalism From Below: Markets and Institutional Change in China by Victor Nee and Sonja Opper is an excellent book that brings together two of Nee's longstanding intellectual efforts: describing the political economy of Chinese development and expanding the theoretical utility of New Institutional Economics. The authors engage with this topic by focusing less on where China is going, and more on how China got to where it is today by developing a strong private sector in the face of significant institutional constraints and disincentives. The title of the book refers to the early generations of Chinese entrepreneurs, mostly in the 1980s and 1990s, who pursued the uncertain path of private enterprise, and then were so successful in this pursuit that they effectively pushed the Chinese state to adapt to them. Thus, the domestic private sector prevailed against state and cultural institutions working against them, and ultimately provided alternative institutions that the state often adopted later.
